title: Fasolada
subtitle: Greek white bean soup with tomato and olive oil
excerpt: A thick, sustaining soup of white beans, tomato, celery, and carrot, finished with a generous pour of olive oil. Known as the national dish of Greece, this is winter cooking at its simplest.
sourceNotes: Fasolada has been the staple Greek winter soup for centuries, documented in Claudia Roden, The Food of Italy (1989) and Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001). The dish appears in virtually identical form across the Balkans and Aegean; the Greek version relies on good olive oil as the finishing element.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. The beans thicken further on reheating; add a splash of water to loosen.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Uses the same total cooking time.

content.text: Fasolada is often called the national dish of Greece, a claim made half in pride and half in practicality: it is cheap, filling, made from things that keep well, and tastes better the longer it sits. The soup is simple: white beans, tomato, carrot, celery, onion, and a great deal of olive oil — and that simplicity is the point.

Use dried beans if you have them and the time (soak overnight, cook for 60 to 90 minutes in fresh water before proceeding). Tinned haricot or cannellini beans give a good result and cut the time to under 30 minutes. Either way, finish the soup with olive oil added off the heat — it doesn't cook in, it sits on top in a pool that you stir through at the table.

items.prepNote: tinned, drained and rinsed (or 300 g dried, soaked overnight)

content.text: Warm 30 ml of the olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the onion, carrot, and celery with a pinch of salt. Cook, stirring occasionally, for 12 to 15 minutes until softened and beginning to colour. Add the garlic and tomato purée and stir for 2 minutes.

Add the tinned tomatoes, stock, beans, oregano, and bay leaves. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook for 25 to 30 minutes (tinned beans) or 60 to 90 minutes (pre-cooked dried beans) until the beans are very tender and the broth has thickened. Crush a few beans against the side of the pan with a spoon to add body. Season and remove the bay leaves.

Remove from the heat. Pour the remaining 50 ml of olive oil into the soup and stir once. Ladle into bowls, scatter with parsley if using, and add another drizzle of olive oil at the table. Serve with good bread for tearing and dipping.

items.cause: Not enough simmering time or the beans weren't crushed for body
items.fix: Continue simmering uncovered for another 15 minutes; crush more beans against the pot wall

items.symptom: Dried beans remain hard after 90 minutes
items.cause: Old dried beans or soaking water was hard
items.fix: Soak overnight and discard the soaking water; very old beans may need up to 2 hours of simmering

items.cause: Insufficient salt or oil not added at the end
items.fix: Season assertively and always finish with a generous pour of extra-virgin olive oil off the heat
Add a pinch of sugar and simmer for another 10 minutes to soften the acidity

content.text: Fasolada was the food of fasting days in the Greek Orthodox calendar (no meat, no dairy), which is why it was cooked so frequently and why it became associated with Greek identity. During the Ottoman period it fed armies and monasteries. Today it is eaten on cold winter evenings alongside olives, pickled vegetables, and a chunk of feta. The soup tastes better the next day, when the beans have had time to absorb the broth fully.
